title: Missing Parentheses in SushiMaker._swap Causes Incorrect Amount Calculation
reason: False positive: Solidity member-call precedence makes the denominator `reserve*.mul(1000).add(amountInWithFee)`, so the swap formula is already parsed correctly.

title: Timelock Allows Arbitrary Contract Execution Without Validation
reason: By design: a timelock intentionally performs arbitrary queued admin actions after delay; requiring admin compromise is not a distinct contract vulnerability.

title: SushiRoll Migration Lacks Slippage Protection for New Pools
reason: Not a clear vulnerability: the function effectively removes liquidity and re-adds it at current reserves while returning leftovers to the caller; no protocol-side loss mechanism is shown.

title: MasterChef Dev Fee Not Transparent
reason: This is a tokenomics/governance choice, not an implementation flaw that creates an exploit path.

title: MasterChef SafeSushiTransfer May Distribute Incorrect Amounts
reason: Not supported as a standalone issue: `safeSushiTransfer` only caps payouts to the contract's actual SUSHI balance to avoid dust/rounding failures and cannot transfer more than it holds.

title: MasterChef Pool Allocation Points Have No Upper Bound
reason: Speculative/non-material: SafeMath protects arithmetic, and no realistic exploit or protocol harm is demonstrated from large owner-set allocation points alone.

title: SushiToken Uses Deprecated `now` Keyword
reason: Code-quality concern only; it does not create a realistic security impact in this deployed Solidity version.

title: SushiBar Enter/Leave Functions Subject to Rounding Loss
reason: Expected integer truncation in share accounting; any loss is negligible and not reportable as a security issue.

title: SushiMaker onlyEOA Modifier Can Be Bypassed Via Smart Contract
reason: Incorrect: `require(msg.sender == tx.origin)` rejects calls made through contracts, including the cited mock, so no bypass is demonstrated.

title: Timelock Receive Function Allows Direct ETH Transfers
reason: Accepting ETH is intentional and recoverable through normal timelock-controlled execution; no adversarial exploit path is shown.
